title: MarginalizationAndIntervention

lean_statement.name: marginalize_hardInterventionOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_18: marginalization and hard intervention commute on
disjoint subsets,
`(G.hardInterventionOn W₁).marginalize W₂
   = (G.marginalize W₂).hardInterventionOn W₁`. Mirrors the
displayed equality of the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 1122. Statement only
at this stage; the proof body is `sorry`, to be discharged by the
`prove_claim_in_lean` worker in the proof-phase manager pass
(Manager B). -/
theorem marginalize_hardInterventionOn (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁).marginalize W₂
      = (G.marginalize W₂).hardInterventionOn W₁ := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states the equality $(G_{\doit(W_1)})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})_{\doit(W_1)}$ for a CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and disjoint subsets $W_1, W_2$ of nodes from $G$. In the Lean encoding, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — the LN leaves this implicit, mentioning only "nodes from $G$" — and `G : CDMG α` corresponds to the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$, a structure bundling the input nodes $J$, the output nodes $V$, the directed edges $E$, and the bidirected edges $L$. The two set arguments `W₁ W₂ : Set α` are the LN's $W_1$ (the intervened set) and $W_2$ (the marginalized set); the explicit hypothesis `h : Disjoint W₁ W₂` is the LN's "disjoint". The operations `G.hardInterventionOn W₁` and `G.marginalize W₂` are the Lean encodings of $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ (def_3_10) and $G^{\sm W_2}$ (def_3_14) respectively.

A few Mathlib idioms are worth flagging. `Disjoint` comes from `Mathlib.Order.Disjoint` — the order-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set α` unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ and ships a built-in `Disjoint.symm` so that a consumer who wants the swapped form can pass `h.symm` without any manual set-theoretic detour. `Set α` is Mathlib's standard type of subsets of `α`, encoded as predicates `α → Prop`. The curly braces around `W₁ W₂` mark them as implicit binders: the elaborator recovers both sets from the type of `h`, so consumers spell only `G` and `h` at the call site.

Shape-wise the theorem takes one explicit CDMG, two implicit subsets recovered from the disjointness witness, and one explicit hypothesis, and concludes a single equation between two CDMGs obtained by composing intervention and marginalization in opposite orders. Notice that the LN's prose preconditions $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ and $W_2 \subseteq V$ do **not** appear as Lean hypotheses; the Lean operators `hardInterventionOn` and `marginalize` are total functions of `Set α`, so the equality holds for arbitrary subsets and the LN's subset clauses are absorbed into the design of the operators rather than carried as hypotheses (see the design notes).
design_choices:
**Single equality, not a fusion-plus-commute pair.** Two close siblings in Section 3.2 — `HardInterventionsCommute.lean` (two hard interventions) and `MarginalizationsCommute.lean` (two marginalizations, claim_3_17) — are each formalised as a fusion lemma (`f W₁ ∘ f W₂ = f (W₁ ∪ W₂)`) plus a commute corollary (`f W₁ ∘ f W₂ = f W₂ ∘ f W₁`). For this row that pattern is unavailable: the two operations being composed are *different* functions on `Set α` (`hardInterventionOn` and `marginalize`), not the same operation composed with itself, and there is no third operation $g$ such that `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.g (W₁ ⊕ W₂)` for any binary `⊕` on `Set α`. The LN's own displayed equation is a single $=$, not a chain, so the natural Lean shape is one theorem rather than a fusion-plus-commute pair.

**No `W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and no `W₂ ⊆ G.V` preconditions.** The LN's prose carries both subset hypotheses, but the Lean encoding drops them. Both component operators are total functions of `Set α` by design (each cites *composition with the other* as a load-bearing motivation for dropping the precondition — this very row is that composition). The four-component equality therefore holds for arbitrary `W₁ W₂ : Set α`, regardless of whether they sit inside `G.J ∪ G.V` or `G.V`. Vertices outside the relevant component of $G$ are silently ignored by both operators on the way to the equality, so the LN's prose preconditions are informal scaffolding rather than load-bearing hypotheses.

**`Disjoint W₁ W₂` is kept as an explicit hypothesis — it is load-bearing.** The LN's own statement carries disjointness, and the LN proof leans on it in the $E$ and $L$ analyses: an interior-in-$W_2$ walk's intermediate nodes must lie outside $W_1$ for the intervention's edge deletions to be transparent to marginalization's walk-existential predicate, and the endpoint-exclusion clause of `hardInterventionOn` only lines up with the shared $G.V \setminus (W_1 \cup W_2)$ of the two sides under disjointness. Drop `h` and the RHS marginalizes nodes that the LHS treats as interventional (and vice versa); the equation outright fails. Encoded via Mathlib's `Disjoint`, so a consumer that wants the swapped form can feed `h.symm` directly.

**Naming `marginalize_hardInterventionOn` (outer-then-inner convention).** Follows Mathlib's `image_image` / `map_map` / `comap_comap` style for swap-of-two-named-ops lemmas: the name reads outer first, then inner. The LHS `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁).marginalize W₂` has `marginalize` outermost and `hardInterventionOn` inside. Alternatives considered: `hardInterventionOn_marginalize` (rejected — inverts the outer/inner reading, a reader expecting the convention would look for `(G.marginalize _).hardInterventionOn _` as the LHS); `marginalize_hardInterventionOn_comm` (rejected — the Mathlib `_comm` suffix is reserved for the arguments-swap of a single binary operator, not for the swap of two different operators); `..._fuse` / `..._fusion` (rejected — there is no fusion here, so a fusion-flavoured name would mislead).

**Argument visibility.** `G : CDMG α` is explicit (the carrier object under study is always spelled at the call site, matching every other CDMG-quantified lemma in this section), `W₁ W₂` are implicit (recovered from `h` to avoid spelling the two sets three times in the binder list), and `h` is explicit (load-bearing and not inferable from the conclusion). The convention of which set is $W_1$ vs $W_2$ follows the LN's left-to-right reading of the LHS $(G_{\doit(W_1)})^{\sm W_2}$: $W_1$ is the intervened set, $W_2$ is the marginalized one.

**The LN's underlying motivation: making $G_{\doit(W_1)}^{\sm W_2}$ an unambiguous notation.** The LN's commented-out remark immediately after the displayed equation observes that the bare notation $G_{\doit(W_1)}^{\sm W_2}$ (no parentheses indicating which transformation is applied first) is well-defined as a CDMG only because the two orderings yield the same CDMG — which is exactly what this lemma proves. Downstream consumers in the do-calculus and identification chapters silently lean on this fact whenever they apply the notation to a graph with both interventions and marginalisations.

**The LN's trailing forward-pointer is not formalised at this row.** The LN's closing sentence — "A similar statement holds for marginalizations and adding intervention nodes, and also for marginalizations and node-splitting interventions" — is a forward-pointer to two analogue commutation lemmas (`marginalize` vs `addInterventionNodes`, `marginalize` vs `nodeSplittingOn`), each of which is a separate numbered row under the project's row-per-statement convention. They are deferred to downstream rows and are not implicitly covered by this theorem.
